Transcrição Completa: 89 Maximizing Productivity 6 8 24
[00:00]
"Welcome to the weekly group call. This week we're going to be going into productivity and maximizing it. A lot of people out there think that they're actualizing on their full potential, but in most instances after a sustained period of time, you recognize that you could do a lot more than whatever you're currently doing now."
[00:25]
"Put in the chat, do you agree or disagree that you can currently do more than what you're doing today? You feel like you can draw more out of yourself than what you're currently bringing out of yourself right now."
[00:40]
"Now, if you had to just immediately pinpoint a variable or a set of variables that hold your productivity back, what would they be? If you had to immediately acknowledge something or a set of things that just hold you back, they're immediately recognizable. What are they? Feel free to unmute yourself and share or drop them in the chat as well."
[01:05]
"I'll share that. For me, I reckon it's just lack of focus, being distracted, doing too many different things."
[01:12]
"Makes sense. What causes the distractions though?"
[01:15]
"Preventable things."
[01:17]
"The thing with productivity is we got to be really specific with ourselves. Okay? It's like what Ivan was just saying about wanting to lose weight. If he's not just hyper specific about the times of day where he might be overeating, you know, or maybe he could use a meal prep service to track his caloric intake rather than ordering everything off of Door Dash."
[01:40]
"Maybe he has no idea the amount of calories that he's burning. You know, these are all measurable things that are specific that allow him to lose weight or gain weight. It's the same thing with productivity. We have to be very, very specific and aware of what makes us productive and what throws us out of being productive, distracts us, holds us back, acts as an anchor."
[02:05]
"So before I even get started on what makes me hyperproductive, I just want to be very clear. We have to start with what distracts us, holds us back, and acts as an anchor for our productivity."
[02:18]
"Let me give you a good example. Ben Bader, unmute yourself. Tell me about going out late at night, clubbing. I assume you drink alcohol. How do you perform those next days?"
[02:30]
"Um, not too great. A bit sluggish."
[02:33]
"Right? But to be clear, like, how old are you right now?"
[02:36]
"23."
[02:37]
"Is this just a phase of your life where you feel you need to do these things and that's where you have the most fun and you prioritize that over the productivity that you essentially lose the following day? So I assume that you're doing this strategically on days where the next day you don't need to actualize on your full productivity. Right?"
[02:55]
"Correct."
[02:56]
"And that in itself is a strategy to maximize productivity. Is it not?"
[03:00]
"Yep."
[03:01]
"We have to pick and choose the days where we will fall victim to what throws us out of our productivity cycles. And we have to also pick and choose when we are going to string a set of days together where we are hyperproductive and we don't do the things that hold us back or distract us."
[03:18]
"Look at the end of the day almost all of you as you put into the chat are very aware of the things that hold you back. Now, the next question is, do you feel in control of those things that hold you back? Do you feel like you can actually do something about it, or do you feel like you're a full-blown victim and you got no control over it?"
[03:38]
"Like, as an example, Ben G, you put overanalyzing. I'll never forget the first time I talked to you. Your brain is a fucking scattered chaotic mess that is just in every possible direction where it's like you're pulling yourself, I can't even imagine in how many different ways within 10 minutes. Do you have control over that?"
[03:58]
"Yeah. No, of course. I mean, everything you have control of. It's just naturally it's all over the place."
[04:08]
"Advantage and disadvantage. You know, to be clear, there's many things you could do to gain more control over that, right?"
[04:14]
"Yeah."
[04:15]
"Think about it like this. What could you do if you had to do something to calm the monkey brain down? Just simplify what is simple task like making an Instagram video like it's honestly a pretty simple fucking task. Just see what works and repeat it don't overthink it."
[04:35]
"Let me give you a good example. What I noticed about myself learning how to trade over this last year is when I have a great day the rest of my day is euphoric it's like I'm on ecstasy versus the days that I lose 40, $60,000. Those days initially could be rather devastating to me and they could fuck up the rest of my day."
[05:00]
"Just from the initial emotional state that I enter into between 9:30 and 11:00 a.m. could really influence the rest of my days just psychology, the way I feel, the amount of money I can make throughout the rest of the day. It's like I either get a lot of momentum from it or I immediately ruin my momentum based on a potential five to two-hour action that I take place 5 days a week every day Monday through Friday on days that are tradable."
[05:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Trading Loss Example
NÚMEROS: $4,500 per account, 19 accounts, $85,500 total loss
VISUAL: Exemplo de perda massiva em trading que normalmente afetaria produtividade do dia inteiro, demonstração de controle emocional
[05:30]
"And just recently, yesterday I was down, let me do the math real quick, it was $4,500 per account, 19 account. So at one point yesterday at about 10:00 a.m. I had lost $85,500."
[05:48]
"Now, normally that would fuck me up. That would make me feel bad. You know, that would throw me through a loop of unproductive feelings and just yuckiness throughout the rest of the entire day. When I'd show up to calls, I'd feel bad. You know, when I would do work, I'd feel bad. I would just be distracted, sluggish. You know, I took a $85,500 hit."
[06:10]
"But instead, I actually didn't feel anything, you know, which was really good. I had gotten control over it and I had throughout the entire year of learning this skill. I'd say for the first eight to nine months not had control over it. And then in the most recent four to five months I have been able to fucking dominate that feeling that would otherwise have held me back."
[06:33] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Recovery From Loss
NÚMEROS: $833 × 19 accounts = $15,827 profit, recovered from -$85,500
VISUAL: Transformação de perda massiva em lucro através de controle emocional, demonstração de produtividade mantida apesar de adversidade inicial
[06:35]
"And yesterday was one of the first times where being down that much I actually ended up, hang on let me do the math on this one. 833 time 19, I ended up plus 15,827 which isn't a huge day but regardless thankfully I didn't lose $85,500 I made $15,827 instead. That's how I finished off the day yesterday."
[07:00]
"So I felt good. You know what I did at the end of the day to reward myself because I didn't feel the unproductiveness. I didn't feel the lack of, you know, money coming in and losing that amount of money affecting the rest of my day. I rewarded myself, you know what I rewarded myself with?"

NÚMEROS: Rose gold Day-Date watch (implied high value)

[07:20]

[07:33]

[07:42]
"Yep."
[07:43]

[07:58]
"We have to create a loop of positive dopamine cycles. Okay? We can't just overcome a boss and then not give ourselves some type of reward. We have to do something that essentially acknowledges to oursel, hey, congratulations, buddy. You did it. You know, you overcame it. Good job."
[08:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Monkey Brain Solution
NÚMEROS: Mark Dhama (high performance coach mentioned)
VISUAL: Conceito de meditação e breath work para controlar pensamentos caóticos, processo de ganhar controle sobre mente dispersa
[08:20]
"Like, as an example, Ben, self-awareness on the fact that your brain is all over the place. I had a high performance coach, guy named Mark Dhama, and Mark taught me a simple lesson when I first started working with him. He called it monkey brain."
[08:35]
"He was like, 'Jeremy, you need to meditate far more often than you do so you can gain control over your otherwise chaotic thoughts. And you need to be able to string together coherent, in control, productive, consistent thought processes that don't get pulled in random directions by different things. And you're going to do this through breath work and meditation.'"
[08:58]
"I think breath work and meditation, to be very transparent, is super gay. I do not like it. However, I do it consistently because it's gotten me the result of having coherent, consistent, strung together, non-distracted thoughts that I'm in control of. In addition to hypnotism, to be fair."
[09:18]
"My point being I had to take an action in order to generate the result to overcome that. Okay? Some things are that simple. We have to identify what holds us back. We have to identify what we can do to overcome it. We have to do the thing that overcomes it sometimes consistently and then we have to reward ourselves on the other side of that after we've overcome it."
[09:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Productivity Cycle
NÚMEROS: 4 passos mencionados
VISUAL: Ciclo completo: 1) Identificar o que segura, 2) Identificar solução, 3) Executar consistentemente, 4) Recompensar-se. Framework em loop para melhoria contínua
[09:42]
"Does that make sense to everybody? Put a yes in the chat if that makes sense. Unmute yourself if you got a question on it. That cycle is very important to understand. Okay? Because if we miss a key part of that cycle, we don't actually make any progress."
[10:00]
"Like if I'm aware of what distracts me or causes me to be unproductive and then I'm aware of what I could do about it, but I don't actually do anything about it. Am I in any way, shape, or form benefiting from that? No."
[10:15]
"I have to then do something about it and then I have to reward myself at the end. Rewarding yourself at the end, once again, very key part of the whole process. Okay? Because once again, you have to understand brain chemistry. We need those brain chemicals to fire off to essentially literally hardwire in our brain that we did a good thing there so we can repeat the behavior easier."
[10:38]
"Without that part of it, we don't get that neural pathway established and we're unfortunately likely to fall victim to it again. It's why the reward part is so important. Does that make sense as well to everybody?"
[10:50]
"Yeah. Fucking yeah."

VISUAL: Conceito de linha de base de produtividade - nível normal consistente de performance que serve como referência para melhorias ou quedas
[10:54]

[11:15]
"All of you are hyper aware of what your normal day-to-day baseline behavior of productivity is. Okay. Some of you might be lower than others. Some of you might be higher than others. But you have to be first of all just aware of your norm."
[11:30]
"If anybody is not aware of your norm, feel free to unmute yourself. Let's dig into it real quick so you can become aware. If you are aware, just do me a favor real quick. Put a one in the chat so I can move on from this."

"Cool. Okay, seeing a bunch of ones. Good."
[11:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Baseline vs Sporadic Increases
NÚMEROS: Não especificado numericamente
VISUAL: Comparação visual entre aumentos esporádicos temporários (picos que voltam à linha de base) vs aumento sustentado da linha de base (novo patamar permanente mais alto)
[11:50]
"So to be clear, once we're aware of our baseline, what can we do from there? Well, first of all, like I just talked about, we can be self-aware when we go below it and we can identify what's pulling us down. But second, and most importantly, we can also start trying to implement all kinds of things that push it up."
[12:10]
"Now, our goal with productivity, I want to be very clear when I say this, our goal with productivity and what I'm going to talk to you about is not to sporadically go above the baseline and then come back down to the baseline. Our goal with what I'm going to talk to you about is to raise the baseline through time so it is normal and doesn't exhaust you to have a heightened level of productivity consistently instead of just random sporadic increases in productivity."
[12:40]
"Does that make sense? We want to increase the baseline to a heightened level rather than just randomly going above it. How many of you feel like you get times where you randomly go above it and you can recognize you snap right back down to the baseline?"
[12:55]
"Cool, right? Maybe it's from like a stimulant, you know? Maybe you'll pop an adderall, drink some fucking coffee or green tea or whatever the fuck you drink, you know? Yeah, cool, right? But not sustainable."
[13:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Stimulant Reality
NÚMEROS: 5-6 years since last Adderall use mentioned
VISUAL: Crítica ao uso de estimulantes como Adderall e café - aumento temporário seguido de queda abaixo da linha de base, não sustentável a longo prazo
[13:12]
"I think as an example, adderall is one of the greatest gifts on God's green earth in terms of productivity, but I can't take that shit every day, you know? I don't take it. I haven't taken adderall in, I don't know, it's been like five, six years where I'd sporadically taken Adderall. It's not sustainable, you know? And in addition to that has lots of side effects."
[13:35]
"Same thing with any stimulant for that matter that you might be reliant upon. You consistently drink coffee. I don't drink coffee. I don't take any kind of stimulant. I don't need to. I've raised my baseline."
[13:48]
"When you don't raise the baseline, you have to consistently rely on sporadic things that pull you up. So, I want to be clear in saying this. We have to also, just as we're aware of distractions, anchors, things that hold us back, we have to be aware of things that lift us from our baseline, but then fucking drop us from that height back to the baseline after."
[14:10]
"We ideally don't want to do those things unless necessary. To be fair, there are days where you might just need to get a fuckload of stuff done and you didn't put in the work to increase your baseline yet to be able to get that amount of stuff done naturally and you need to rely on something. Arguably, I'd say that's fair. However, we don't want to do that every day, right?"

[15:33] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Stimulant Crash Pattern
NÚMEROS: Não especificado numericamente
VISUAL: Padrão de estimulantes: janela curta de baseline elevado, seguida por crash abaixo do baseline normal. Questão: produtivo após crash? Resposta: Não, abaixo do baseline
[15:35]
"So stimulants typically have short windows of heightened baseline, right? For those who drink coffee or take some kind of mental stimulant, how long does it usually last? And what happens after you crash?"
[15:48]
"That's right."
[15:50]
"Are you productive after the crash?"
[15:52]
"Nope."
[15:53]
"Does it crash back to the baseline or below the baseline?"
[15:56]
"Oh, below for sure."
[15:58]
"Is that a good productive consistent thing to do based on that logic? If it increases us short-term and then decreases us below our normal baseline after?"
[16:08]
"It defeats the purpose."
[16:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Brandon Carter Exception
NÚMEROS: 400-500mg caffeine daily
VISUAL: Único exemplo de uso consistente e bem-sucedido de estimulantes - Brandon Carter com 400-500mg cafeína diária através de pílulas (não café), mantendo baseline consistente sem permitir crash
[16:12]
"That's right. There's also a lot of downsides to long-term stimulant usage. I will say there's only one person I've ever met that consistently is productive on stimulants that I've met and that's Brandon Carter. But he supplements 400 to 500 milligrams of caffeine a day and he doesn't do it through coffee. He does it through literal pills."
[16:35]
"And he's when I say consistent I mean he doesn't allow the crash to occur. You know he consistently will take the caffeine throughout the day and he has a very consistent baseline as a result of that amount of stimulants that he's consuming."
[16:50]
"I wouldn't recommend anybody personally to take 400 to 500 milligrams of caffeine per day. I don't view that as sustainable through time. I think that that's rather unhealthy to a degree, but you know, we'll find out. I could be wrong and Brandon could be right. That's the beauty of life."
[17:10]
"Point is, everybody has a little different process that you put yourself through to get yourself temporarily above baseline. So, let me talk to you about the things specifically that I think through time have consistently worked my baseline up to a higher level, allowed me to cut out any form of stimulants, allowed me to be at an excessively heightened level of energy consistently."

[17:38]
"Okay, number one, this one's in my opinion one of the most important. Environment. Environment feeds you or it eats away at you. It's really no in between. From my experience, your environment is a benevolent, incredible source of energy, productivity, feeds you or it takes away from you. It's as simple as that. And you got to be very aware of it."
[18:05]
"Give you a great example. Most of you right now are talking to me from some house that you live in or an apartment of some kind. Some of you right now might be traveling. Ask yourself this just in terms of your home environment. Does it feed you? Does it take away from you? Think about the baseline. You know, when you moved into wherever you live at now, did it lift the baseline? Did it lower the baseline? Did it leave it at where it was?"

[24:58]
"Me I like to live a concept I call the compound life. I found this highly beneficial. You can do this at apartments. I prefer to do it at a house. I first started doing this when I was at my penthouse. It had a basketball court, a tennis court, a gym. It was on an island. It had obviously been surrounded by water, so I could kayak, jet ski, boat within about a two-minute walk from where I lived."
[25:25]

I don't ever enter into flow state in random environments. There are occasions where I've entered into flow state in random environments, but it's very rare."
[30:58]

[41:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Environmental Assimilation Principle
NÚMEROS: Não especificado numericamente
VISUAL: Princípio central: assimilamos aos ambientes. Ambiente de produtividade = assimila produtividade e eleva baseline. Ambiente de riqueza/paz = assimila esses valores (roupas, relógios, veículos diferentes)
[41:45]
"If we have an environment that we set up that is intended to maximize productivity, we assimilate to that and it raises our baseline. When we are in an environment that in this case, like I described to you, my second home, it's like you assimilate to being wealthier, you assimilate to being more peaceful, you assimilate to dressing a different way, you assimilate to wearing different types of watches, you assimilate to different types of vehicles that you own as a result of environmental influence."
[42:15]
"You get what I'm saying? The state you live in, the city you live in, the country you live in, all have underlying things that whisper to you how you need to be and what you need to do."

[42:45]

[43:22]

[43:42]

I learned that Steve Jobs, more importantly, I learned that Dr. Dre and I learned that Zuckerberg all do this too. I thought it was pretty cool."
[44:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Dr. Dre's Wardrobe System
NÚMEROS: 3 cores de camiseta, 3 cores de manga longa, 1 tipo de shorts, 1 tipo de jeans
VISUAL: Sistema de guarda-roupa do Dr. Dre: 3 cores da mesma camiseta, 3 cores da mesma manga longa, mesmo shorts, mesmo jeans, variações mínimas em sapatos. Decisões reduzidas a: manga curta ou longa? Qual cor? Calça ou shorts? Quais sapatos?
[44:08]
"I'll use Dr. Dre as an example because that's the one that really influenced me to do this. Dr. Dre has three colors of the exact same t-shirt and three colors of the exact same long sleeve shirt. He has the exact same pair of shorts and the exact same pair of jeans."
[44:25]
"And he has slight variations in his shoes that he'll wear, but he has very consistent shoes that he'll consistently wear. He'll have random shoes for specific occasions otherwise. So, his wardrobe that he has to pick through is okay, is it a long sleeve shirt or a short sleeve shirt today? Do I want to wear this color or that color? Okay, great. There's your shirt. Do I want to wear these pants or these shorts? Okay, great. I wear these shoes or these shoes. Okay, great. Done."

[44:55]

[45:15]
"And there's different types of decisions obviously, but the little ones, those things add up to a pretty big reduction of that bar's overall energy for the rest of the day. Okay? And little things add up."

[46:48]
"I have the same thing for breakfast every single morning. It's a smoothie. Has a multivitamin in it. Has a fuckload of berries. I do really well with carbohydrates. I don't do that well on the keto stuff. But to be clear, this my daily little breakfast that I have. Gives me about 350 calories, which from what I've experienced is the perfect amount to get me till about 1 to two in the afternoon where I'll then make a decision on what I'd like for lunch."
[47:18]
"Lunch and dinner, I'm willing to vary what I consume. To be clear, I don't have a consistent lunch or dinner that I eat."
[47:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Complete Daily Outfit
NÚMEROS: ~40 pares de sapatos não usados, Balenciaga sock shoes como favorito absoluto
VISUAL: Outfit diário completo: Calvin Klein underwear (mesma), Daniel Patrick shirt (preto ou cinza?), Champion shorts (mesmos), Balenciaga sock shoes (mesmos). 40+ outros sapatos ignorados. Decisão da manhã eliminada
[47:30]
"I wear the same shorts every day. These are those Champion shorts. I don't have any difference in shorts. I wear the exact same shorts every single day, no matter what. So, my outfit choices, the exact same pair of underwear, Calvin Klein. I got the exact same shirt. Is it black or is it gray? And I got the exact same pair of shorts."
[47:52]
"So, immediately in the morning, I've reduced what I have to think about. You'll obviously see me wearing those little Balenciaga sock shoes all the time. It's my favorite pair of shoes by far. I probably have 40 other pairs of shoes that don't see the light of day. You know, they just don't."
[48:10]
"It's not because I don't like them. It's not because I don't want to wear them. It's literally just because most of the time I look at it as a worthless decision that I'm not going to allow to take away from my overall decision-making bar in a day."
[48:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Save Energy for Important Decisions
NÚMEROS: Não especificado numericamente
VISUAL: Estratégia: preservar "juice" da barra de decisão para decisões importantes que afetam produtividade, finanças, vida. Não desperdiçar em roupas ou café da manhã
[48:28]
"That way come time when the important decisions come up, I have enough juice to actually make them properly. Okay? I don't want to get caught off guard at the times that I need to make proper decisions that relate to my productivity and that benefit me financially or that benefit me in life in general and have a reduction in the bar because I spent too much time that morning picking out what fucking clothes I want to wear or what I'm going to eat for breakfast that day."
[48:55]
"If you do have a wide variance on the amount of clothes you could wear and the combinations of clothes or what you're going to consume for what meals as an example, just make those decisions rapidly. Don't let those things draw out on you for long durations of time. Just try to work through those things fast. That's the lesson."
[49:15]
"To be clear, I'd love for you guys to give some feedback on what feeds you, what you guys find beneficial, what you find as an anchor. I'd love to hear some feedback from you guys real quick before I continue. Feel free to unmute yourself and share. Somebody go."
[49:32]
"House car cigar music."
[49:36]
"Adrian, what about you? What do you got? Top of the list."

[52:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Wins vs Losses Impact
NÚMEROS: Não especificado numericamente
VISUAL: Win streaks = baseline aumenta consistentemente. Losing streaks = baseline diminui progressivamente. Importância de criar wins ativos (físicos, mentais, espirituais, dinheiro, pessoais, família)
[52:20]
"Okay. So, number one, wins and losses. I don't know about you guys, but whenever I'm on a win streak, I feel much more of my baseline, like feeling like it consistently increases versus whenever I'm on a consistent losing streak, I just I don't know. I feel like I can do less and less and less the more that my losses continue to compound."
[52:45]
"So, I actively try to create wins as much as possible for myself. And wins are interpreted by everybody different, but wins can count as physical wins, mental wins, spiritual wins, money wins, personal wins, family wins. I mean literally anything guys."
[53:05]
"It's very important that we acknowledge and like I said reward oursel when we experience wins that we will want to perpetually win. I'm not devastated in the instance I experience a loss to be clear like my entire mental state's not contingent on consistent victories. I just recognize simply put that I get a heightened level of productivity when I have consistent victories."
[53:30]
"So I attempt to push for that but my identity is not built on it to be fair. That's also a very important variable here. We can't have our entire identities built around these different variables. We just need to recognize once again what benefits us and what deters and detracts from us in comparison."

She literally checks every box on my list. She's somebody I could talk to that I benefit from in conversations with."
[54:15]

Once again that you just have like in your phones that you're talking with like once again do things that feed you deter from the things that pull away."
[54:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Nuances & Annoyances

VISUAL: Computador que laga = raiva extrema, internet lenta = caos. Solução: linha fiber optic dedicada em ambas as casas, 10 gigs de dados, hardwired no router, melhores devices Apple sempre (soup up ao máximo)
[54:40]
"Last thing nuances and annoyances. So nuances and annoyances they count for a lot of things but I'll give you a great example. Like I get so pissed if I have a computer that lags in any way, shape, or form or if my internet is slow."
[54:58]
"As an example, like I'm an advertiser. If I have to download video ads and then upload them into the ads manager and it takes more than like two minutes, I just I'm like I don't know. I just go chaotic. I'm like this is taking way too fucking long. You shouldn't."
[55:15]
"So I have a dedicated fiber optic line ran to both of my houses. Okay. Not that expensive to be fair, but regardless, you know, it's much more expensive than regular Wi-Fi. But to be clear, nobody's going to fuck with my internet connection. My videos download and upload lightning speed. It's amazing to experience 10 gigabytes of fucking data."
[55:38]

I consistently buy the best devices possible. So, anytime Apple comes out with a new superco computer, I buy that shit and I soup it up to the max."
[56:00]

[56:40]

[57:22]

[57:35]
"Nuances and annoyances. Figure out what the fuck they are for you and cut them out. Fix them. Spend the money to maximize the opposite of that, which are the things that put you into flow state easier, the things that make you feel good, the things that once again for some of you within control. You know, it's different for everybody."
[57:58]
"And to be clear, focus on the wins, focus on avoiding the losses. For me, that's once again a pretty big one. Focus on the right people. These all make a substantial difference."
[58:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Eliminate Time Wasters
NÚMEROS: 4 carros (para nunca ficar sem quando 1 está em manutenção)
VISUAL: Exemplo: carro quebrado = tow truck pega, não vê o carro durante conserto, tem vários outros para usar. Ter apenas 1 carro seria nuisance/annoyance. Staff problemático = conversa direta de accountability
[58:12]
"To be clear, if there's anything that takes time away from you in a day that is just unproductive, by literal definition, that's below your baseline. So, it's like, if something happens to one of my cars, do you guys think I drive it to the fucking dealership, sit in the dealership while they fix it, and then drive it home after? No. No. No. Absolutely no."
[58:35]
"You know, I get a tow truck that comes, picks it up. I don't even see the car or give a fuck about the car while it's gone because I have several other cars I can drive in the meantime. It would be an annoyance and a nuance to only have one car in the instance that I need a car and something goes wrong with it, would it not?"
[58:52]
"A nuance and annoyance would be having one car, so therefore I have four. You know, at one point I had much more than that, but now I have all the cars that I want."
[59:05]
"To be clear, just nuance and annoyances. If people that you have to work with are causing themselves to essentially be a nuance and an annoyance, it's important to note like you know people are their own people. You don't get to influence and control other people's behavior."
[59:22]
"But it is important to note you can have a direct conversation with them in an attempt to improve their behavior in the instance that you do have influence or control over it, especially if they're a staff member. I do that with clients all the time. If they do shit that holds them back, that just pisses me off, annoys me, makes me feel like it's a loss, I go have a direct conversation with them and I hold them accountable."
[59:45]
"You know, but to be fair, it's to benefit them and benefit me at the same time. But regardless, you know, I don't let people stand in the way. That's the moral of the story. I maximize my baseline. Okay?"
[59:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Recovery - The Missing Piece
NÚMEROS: 25 anos quando começou tracking, HRV de pessoa de 90-100 anos, 8-9 meses sem controle emocional, 4-5 meses com controle
VISUAL: Recovery ignorada nos 20s, consequências severas. HRV (heart rate variability) de 90-100 anos aos 25. Blood work péssimo (ex-vegan). Dispositivos de tracking inicialmente pensados quebrados
[60:00]
"And then the last thing, recovery. This one I had never put any attention on, especially when I was in my young 20s. I sure wish I did. To be clear, my recovery was so fucked by the time I started tracking it, we thought all my recovery related tracking devices were broken."
[60:20]
"I had the HRV, which is heart rate variability. It's an indication of your nervous system state. I had the HRV of a 90 to 100 year old man at 25. When I started tracking my health related statistics, my blood work was fucked you know, because I was a vegan for a sustained period of time, which I do not recommend."

Okay, that's just a singular example of it. There are many different examples of it that you can experience."
[61:05]

[61:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Whoop Band & HRV Tracking
NÚMEROS: Não especificado numericamente
VISUAL: Whoop band para tracking de HRV. HRV = indicação se sistema nervoso está realmente desligado durante sono para restaurar corpo física e mentalmente. HRV baixo = produtividade baseline naturalmente mais baixa no(s) dia(s) seguinte(s)
[61:20]
"To be clear, I wear a whoop band to track my heart rate variability. Heart rate variability, the reason it matters, it's an indication of whether my nervous system is actually disengaged when it needs to disengage in order to restore my body physically and mentally."
[61:40]
"If I fail to have a high HRV, I can notice undoubtedly in the following day or unfortunately days that my productivity baseline would naturally be lowered right off the rip based on my quality of sleep, my length of sleep, whether my nervous system was actually able to repair and restore my body while I was sleeping."
[62:02]
"And to be clear, in addition to that, obviously food, what I consume, those all make a substantial difference to my natural baseline. We have to invest in productivity."
[62:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Recovery Equipment Portfolio

[62:18]

[62:40]

[62:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Infrared Mat - Nervous System Switch
NÚMEROS: Não especificado numericamente
VISUAL: Infrared mat como dispositivo físico para mudar de simpático (alerta, engajado) para parassimpático (desligado, recuperando). Usado antes de dormir ou em momentos de peak stress
[62:52]
"I also want to give a huge shout out to my infrared mat. My infrared mat has helped me as a physical device to switch from sympathetic to parasympathetic nervous system state. Sympathetic is what we're all in right now while we're alert, aware, talking to each other, engaged."
[63:12]
"Parasympathetic is where we're disengaged, recovering, long, drawn out breaths, peace, you know, relaxed. So, the infrared mat helps me if in the instance I can't disengage myself, it's just a physical device that allows me to disengage typically before bed or in a moment of peak stress."
[63:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Blood Work Strategy
NÚMEROS: A cada 3 meses (blood work frequency), 25 anos (idade quando começou)
VISUAL: Blood work através de performance lab (não médico regular) para comparar com estatísticas de atleta. Analisa deficiências de vitaminas, níveis hormonais, hormônios de recovery. Ajustes via peptides, injectables, supplements, mudanças de rotina
[63:38]
"I'd encourage all of you to do your blood work, not through a doctor, but through a performance lab. That way you can analyze yourself against more of an athlete set of body composition and blood work statistics. That way you can analyze what is potentially a deficiency in terms of vitamins that you can supplement or potentially hormone levels or all kinds of other recovery related hormones."
[64:05]
"To be clear, there's a lot we can do nowadays through peptides, injectables, supplements, or just changing of our routines in order to maximize our blood work statistics. And that also substantially improves our baseline when we are in a peak state physically from a blood work perspective."
[64:25]
"Don't sleep on that one. I do my blood work every 3 months and I adjust accordingly relative to what I need to supplement or sometimes I just sustain what I'm doing because it's good and it stays good. It's different for everybody, but I didn't start doing blood work till I was 25. I sure as hell wish I would have done it a lot sooner."
[64:48]
"Anybody else have anything they want to add? Any kind of what did I miss?"
[64:55]
"Oh, blue light blockers."
[64:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Circadian Rhythm Tracking
NÚMEROS: 9:30 AM - 11:00 AM (trading window mencionado anteriormente), pôr do sol = tempo de transição
VISUAL: Tracking de ritmo circadiano: manhã/tarde = cortisol alto (stress hormone) = mais alerta/engajado. Pôr do sol = cortisol diminui naturalmente. Pós-pôr do sol sem blue light = produção de melatonina para sono de qualidade
[65:00]
"Oh, yeah, dude. Blue blockers for sure. So, tracking your circadian rhythm. I don't want to forget that one at all. So, when the sun comes up typically through the afternoon at its peak state, our cortisol levels aka our stress hormone is also typically at its peak. That's usually when we have our most engaged and alert states."
[65:22]
"So, I stack my work that is typically the hardest that I need to be the most engaged in during the periods of time relative to my circadian rhythm where I'm typically going to be the most alert and engaged anyway. I try to have more disengaged work later into the day as the sun is setting."
[65:42]
"This might sound a little hippie-ish, but it is fucking awesome when you actually track your circadian rhythm properly. You are naturally supposed to have a decreased level of cortisol literally as the sun is setting. And then to be clear, after the sun sets, as long as you're not tripped out with blue light, your body's supposed to be producing melatonin so you can go to bed naturally and sleep and have a recovery related sleep that's actually good."
[66:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Swannies Blue Light Glasses
NÚMEROS: Não especificado numericamente
VISUAL: Swannies (blue light glasses) usados imediatamente após pôr do sol. Shout out para James (criador). Importante para não interromper produção natural de melatonina
[66:12]
"For me. And this was a big one for sure. The blue light glasses as soon as the sun goes down. I wear swanies to be clear. Really effective and you know shout out to James for creating that product."
[66:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Daily Work Stacking Strategy
NÚMEROS: Não especificado numericamente
VISUAL: Estratégia de empilhamento de trabalho: mais estressante/taxante no início/meio do dia. Progressivamente menos estressante/mais monótono conforme dia avança. Desengajar à medida que dia continua
[66:30]
"But I just want to be clear as well in terms of my workload. I try to do things that are less stressful literally that are just more like monotonous or you know not as taxing on my mental state or my physical state as the day continues to go on."
[66:48]
"So simply put, stack your day to have the most stress-oriented events at the beginning and middle and then try to disengage as the day continues to go on. And make sure that you remember after the sun goes down, even though to be fair, I've done a lot of great work after the sun goes down historically in my career."
[67:08]
"You know, it's not good for your circadian rhythm to increase or sustain high cortisol levels, especially after the sun goes down. You need to track your circadian rhythm. It'll help you a lot to also play into your full-blown recovery and restorative states that you could otherwise be in. Helps a lot."
[67:28]
"So anyway, kind of keep all these things in mind. What else did I miss? Anything? Anything anybody wants to add that you think is an obvious one for you?"

[69:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Content Influence Case Study
NÚMEROS: 5-6 anos (tempo sem Adderall mencionado anteriormente)
VISUAL: Case study: amigo rico com namorada nova obcecada com reality shows (housewives). Comportamento dela começou a emular shows de drama/gossip. Pessoas evitavam estar perto dela. Comportamento inconscientemente influenciado por consumo de conteúdo
[69:02]
"One of my rich friends, he got a new girlfriend and she was hyper obsessed with that set of reality shows like the housewives shows. I'm not familiar with the names of them, but like they just track like the housewives of rich dudes in different parts of the country and they're like drama gossip type shows, you know."
[69:25]
"And long story short, we noticed very quickly that this girl just had a lot of tendencies that were very similar to like what you'd likely expect on one of these shows. And you know, unknowingly to her, her behavior was being influenced dramatically by just continuously watching these shows and emulating that behavior in her own life, causing a lot of like frictions and just, you know, like an unwillingness to want to be around this girl because once again, that was what she was consistently exposing herself to."
[69:55]
"And the opposite is also true. You know, we can watch content that feeds our way that we want to be, you know, and how we want to see ourselves in the world. And that's a powerful thing to leverage. Yeah, by all means."
[70:10]
"Cool. Anything else?"
[70:15]
"All right. Sweet."
[70:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Final Summary - Baseline Philosophy
NÚMEROS: Não especificado numericamente
VISUAL: Resumo final: Conheça seu baseline. Não faça coisas superficiais que aumentam temporariamente e depois caem. Trabalhe consistentemente em ações/variáveis ambientais que AUMENTAM o baseline. Seja consciente do que BAIXA o baseline
[70:20]
"Look, the moral of the story is this the most important lesson here. Know your baseline. Don't do superficial things that shortterm sporadically increase and then bring you back or below your baseline. Try to take and consistently work in the actions or the environmental variables or just the variables in general that increase the baseline that bring more out of you."
[70:45]
"Try to get rid of or at least be self-aware of the things that lower your baseline or at least ideally temporarily take you below your baseline. I don't sit here and encourage all of you to completely take out every distraction that's in your life. You know, I'm not encouraging you to not be a person, you know, and just be like a fucking robot that's hyperfocused on productivity all the time."
[71:10]
"You're naturally going to do things that are going to distract you or just, you know, lower your overall baseline temporarily, ideally, but at the end of the day, do those things strategically when they're not going to influence or corrupt the following day's baseline of productivity."
[71:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Compound Baseline Increases
NÚMEROS: Não especificado numericamente
VISUAL: Objetivo: empilhar variáveis consistentes e compostas na vida para experimentar baseline elevado. Não buscar aumentos temporários que caem de volta. Construir e MANTER baseline elevado (exemplo do café que sobe e desce vs construção sustentada)
[71:30]
"Try to stack yourself up with enough compounded, consistent variables that are within your life to experience a heightened baseline. That's truly how you maximize productivity in comparison to just short-term temporarily supplementing increasing the baseline and then taking it back down to where it was."
[71:50]
"Coffee is an example once again of goes up comes back down. We don't want to do things like that. We want to look for the things once again that consistently build it up and keep it up."
[72:02]
"Okay, cool. Hope that helps. I'll upload the recording when it's done. You guys have more questions after this, you let me know. And per usual, thank you guys for being here. Great talking with you, Bud."
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$85,500 perda em trading revertida para $15,827 lucro no mesmo dia
$19,000/mês aluguel da casa principal
12,000 sq ft casa principal em 5 acres com 5 cavalos
~30 Sonos speakers na casa principal, 6 na segunda casa
70 camisetas idênticas Daniel Patrick (preto e cinza)
350 calorias no smoothie matinal (dura até 1-2 PM)
~40 pares de sapatos não utilizados
4 carros para nunca ficar sem transporte
$5-10k investimento em cold plunge
400-500mg cafeína diária (Brandon Carter - único caso de sucesso com estimulantes)
HRV de pessoa de 90-100 anos aos 25 anos (antes de recovery optimization)
Blood work a cada 3 meses
Segunda casa: 1,800 sq ft, ~0.25 acres, Coconut Grove
$2-5M townhouses, $10-50M casas waterfront na área da segunda casa
2020: ano da decisão sobre decision fatigue
10 gigabytes de internet fiber optic dedicada
1 minuto para acessar cold plunge/sauna/piscina
10 minutos total do cold plunge de volta ao trabalho
5-6 anos desde último uso de Adderall
8-9 meses sem controle emocional no trading
4-5 meses com controle emocional dominado
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Productivity Baseline Philosophy:
Conhecer baseline atual de produtividade
Evitar aumentos esporádicos temporários (estimulantes)
Focar em elevar o baseline permanentemente
Identificar o que alimenta vs o que drena
Ciclo de Superação de Obstáculos:
Identificar o que segura
Identificar solução
Executar consistentemente
Recompensar-se (criar loop dopaminérgico)
Decision Fatigue Elimination:
Roupas idênticas diariamente (70 camisetas, mesmos shorts, mesma cueca)
Café da manhã consistente (smoothie 350 cal)
Dry cleaning automático
Eliminar micro-decisões desnecessárias
Preservar "barra de energia de decisão" para decisões importantes
Compound Life Concept:
Trazer todos os serviços para casa (Instacart, médicos mobile, dentista, quiroprático, massagista, IV therapy)
Ter equipamentos de recovery em casa (cold plunge, sauna, steam room, gym, red light therapy, PEMF mat)
Eliminar tempo de deslocamento
Permanecer em ambientes controlados que alimentam
Dual Environment Strategy:
Casa 1: Produtividade/dinheiro (12k sq ft, isolada, arte de dinheiro)
Casa 2: Recovery/paz/família (1.8k sq ft, walkable, plantas, social)
Ambientes puxam versões diferentes da pessoa
Environmental Control System:
~30 Sonos speakers (música controlada por telefone)
Todas luzes Philips Hue (controle total de vibe)
Arte temática de dinheiro (reflexão de objetivos)
Controle total sobre pessoas no ambiente
Flow State Optimization:
Ambientes consistentes e controlados (não aleatórios)
Múltiplas opções de trabalho (sentar, ficar de pé, caminhar, bike)
Treadmill desk e stationary bike
Capacidade de mudar música, luz, setting à vontade
Recovery Investment Strategy:
Whoop band para tracking HRV
Blood work a cada 3 meses (performance lab, não médico regular)
Infrared mat para switch simpático → parassimpático
Cold plunge, infrared sauna, steam room, red light therapy
Exercise with oxygen equipment, PEMF mat
Circadian Rhythm Tracking:
Trabalho mais difícil durante pico de cortisol (manhã/tarde)
Trabalho menos taxante conforme dia avança
Blue light blockers (Swannies) após pôr do sol
Philips Hue para reduzir blue light à noite
Content Curation Strategy:
Instagram: apenas clientes e conteúdo que condiciona pensamentos desejados
Twitter/X: conteúdo intelectual
TikTok: alguma randomness permitida
Evitar conteúdo que induz comportamentos indesejados
ESTRUTURA:
Hierarquia de Impacto na Produtividade:
Ambiente (feeds you ou eats away - sem meio termo)
Recovery (sono, HRV, blood work, equipamentos)
Decision Fatigue (eliminar micro-decisões)
Pessoas (parceira, pessoas no ambiente)
Nuances & Annoyances (internet, computador, cadeira, etc)
Wins vs Losses (criar wins ativos)
Conteúdo consumido
Framework de Melhorias:
Ações grandes: 5-10% melhoria de benchmark
Ações pequenas: 1-3% melhoria de benchmark
Exemplos 1-3%: arte, música, luzes
Compounding de pequenas melhorias eleva baseline significativamente
Tipos de Wins:
Físicos
Mentais
Espirituais
Dinheiro
Pessoais
Família
Estados de Sistema Nervoso:
Simpático: alerta, engajado, trabalhando
Parassimpático: desligado, recuperando, peace
Infrared mat ajuda transição
Tipos de Ambientes Necessários:
Peak productivity
Recovery extrema (oposto igual em intensidade)
Variação para puxar trabalhos diferentes
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
$19,000/mês: aluguel casa principal
$85,500: perda em trading revertida
$15,827: lucro final do dia de trading
$5,000-10,000: range de cold plunge units
$100,000: relógio flashy (não usado na casa 2)
$2,000,000-5,000,000: townhouses Coconut Grove
$10,000,000-50,000,000: casas waterfront Coconut Grove
Rose gold Day-Date: recompensa por superar controle emocional (valor não especificado)
1970s vintage Submariner: tipo de relógio para segunda casa
Ford Bronco antigo/Defender restaurado: tipo de veículo considerado para segunda casa
Investimentos em Produtividade:
70 camisetas Daniel Patrick
~30 Sonos speakers (casa 1) + 6 (casa 2)
Philips Hue em todas as luzes
Fiber optic dedicado (ambas casas)
Cold plunge ($5-10k)
Infrared sauna
Steam room
Red light therapy
PEMF mat
Exercise with oxygen equipment
Whoop band
Treadmill desk
Stationary bike com rodas
Standup desk
Melhor cadeira de escritório
Melhores devices Apple sempre
Golf cart (casa 2)
4 carros (nunca ficar sem)
Blood work a cada 3 meses (performance lab)
Swannies blue light blockers
Infrared mat
Dry cleaning service 2x/semana
DURAÇÃO: ~72 minutos
FORMATO: Group coaching call sobre maximização de produtividade, apresentação educacional com participação de membros, Q&A interativo, exemplos pessoais detalhados
